questions.question_text: Can I borrow your ............ Hat?
questions.explanation: Adjective Order

questions.question_text: She will not give us ........... more money.
questions.explanation: Any is used when the exact number or amount of something is not known, or when it's not important. Any is also used to refer to an indefinite quantity or number. Any is also used in negative sentences.

questions.question_text: You would be hungry if you ..... to eat.
questions.explanation: would + if should be followed by a past tense

questions.question_text: You have seen them, .... you?
questions.explanation: Have goes with haven't

questions.question_text: Habib goes to school ......... train.
questions.explanation: We travel/go by ...

questions.question_text: She does not like ........ letters.
questions.explanation: When hate,like, love, prefer are used, the verb that follows has ing

questions.question_text: You will be ill if you ....... raw meat.
It was done according .......... the rules.
questions.explanation: According to the rules

questions.question_text: ............... the challenges, we succeeded.
questions.explanation: Despite or in spite of the challenges. Despite and in spite of mean "notwithstanding", "even though","regardless of"

questions.question_text: My brother, with his wife and children,........... travelling to Salaga next weekend
questions.explanation: There is comma after my brother, which means that the one speaking is referring to only the brother hence the singular (is) is used and not are .

questions.question_text: It is high time we ...... the journey.
questions.explanation: Whenever high time is used, its followed by a verb in the past tense . E.g. It is high time we left, it is high time we went etc.

questions.question_text: I prefer oranges ........... mangoes.
questions.explanation: The preposition for prefer is to . He/she prefers .... to .....

questions.question_text: His aunt ....... spoke to us lives in Tamale.
questions.explanation:
When to use who or whom 

 Who : should be used to refer to the subject of a sentence.

 Whom should be used to refer to the object of a verb or preposition.

The subject in a sentence refers to the person or thing carrying out an action. In this example the aunt is the subject since she was the one

questions.question_text: Araba couldn't resist ....... the food while on the fire.
questions.explanation: When couldn't is used, the second verb must be in the ing form. E.g I couldn't resist shar ing it. I couldn't stop laugh ing .

questions.question_text: My neighbour, ............ dog barks every night, has left town.
questions.explanation:
This is possession (the neighbor owns a dog) so whose and not who , possessive pronoun ( whose ) is the correct answer

SECTION B

 Choose from the alternatives lettered A to D the one which is nearest in meaning to the underlined word in each sentence.

questions.question_text: The chosen poems are very relatable
questions.explanation: Chosen nearest in meaning : select, pick, pick out, opt for, plump for, go for, take, settle on, decide on, fix on, come down in favour of, vote for, single out, hand-pick, set, designate, determine, specify, appoint, name, nominate, adopt, espouse

questions.question_text: Arming the police does not halt crime.
questions.explanation: Halt nearest in meaning: stop, come to a halt, come to a stop, come to a standstill, come to rest, pull up, draw up, stand still, draw to a stand, cease, finish

questions.question_text: All the lax rules have been cancelled.
questions.explanation:
Lax: not sufficiently strict, severe, or careful.

 Mild: not severe, serious, or harsh

 Lax nearest in meaning :mild,slack, slipshod, negligent, neglectful, remiss, careless, heedless, unmindful, inattentive, slapdash, offhand, casual, easy-going, lenient, permissive, soft, liberal, non-restrictiv

questions.question_text: The youth should be ruled by the fundamental ideals of courtesy.
questions.explanation: Fundamental nearest in meaning :basics, essentials, rudiments, foundations, basic principles, first principles, preliminaries, crux, essence, core, nucleus, heart, base

questions.question_text: The porter could not endure his insults
questions.explanation: Endure nearest in meaning: , undergo, go through, live through, experience, meet, encounter, cope with, deal with, face, face up to, handle, suffer, tolerate, put up with, brave, bear, withstand, sustain, weather, become reconciled to, reconcile oneself to, become resigned to, get used to, become ac

questions.question_text: Azara burn her fingers when she took sides with her friend. This means that Azara
questions.explanation: To burn one's finger : to harm oneself; to suffer consequences of one's actions.

questions.question_text: Gil held her tongue on sensitive political issues. This means that she
questions.explanation: To hold one's tongue : remain silent

questions.question_text: The project is in the pipeline . This means that it is
questions.explanation: In the pipeline : in the process of being planned or developed.

questions.question_text: Ammon has always kept his enemies at arm's length . This means that he
questions.explanation:
At arm's length : avoid/shun intimacy or close contact.

SECTION D

 From the list of words lettered A to D , choose the one that is most nearly opposite in meaning to the word underlined in each sentence.

questions.question_text: That shop has been temporarily closed down.
questions.explanation: Opposite of temporary is permanent

questions.question_text: He normally alights at the station.
questions.explanation: Opposite of alight is board

questions.question_text: Abeiku feels unsafe in his house
questions.explanation: Opposite of unsafe is secure

questions.question_text: The audience laughed at his funny stories.
questions.explanation: Anything funny is humourous and so if not funny then humourless

questions.question_text: The prefect rebuked the student for his rude behaviour
questions.explanation:
Opposite of rebuke/condemn/disapprove is praise
